data.title: Is It Time To Reassess IHS Holding (IHS) After Its 72% One Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether IHS Holding at US$8.25 is still offering value after its recent run, this breakdown is designed to help you frame that question clearly.
  • The stock has been fairly steady over the last week and month, with returns of 0.0% over 7 days and 0.5% over 30 days. However, the longer term picture includes a 12.2% return year to date and 72.2% over the past year, alongside an 11.7% decline over three years.
  • Recent coverage of IHS Holding has focused on its position within the telecom tower space and how investors are assessing the trade off between its growth profile and sector specific risks. This background helps explain why some shareholders are reassessing what counts as a reasonable price for the stock today.
  • Right now, IHS Holding scores a 5 out of 6 valuation score. The rest of this article will unpack what that means across different valuation methods, before finishing with a broader way to think about what “fair value” really is.

IHS Holding delivered 72.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Telecom industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: IHS Holding Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s value. For IHS Holding, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach that focuses on cash available to shareholders.

IHS Holding has last twelve month free cash flow of about $584.3 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections put free cash flow in 2030 at around $461.1 million, with a series of annual estimates between 2026 and 2035 that are discounted back to present value. All these forecasts are expressed in US$ and Simply Wall St extrapolates beyond the analyst horizon to complete the 10 year path.

When these projected cash flows are discounted and added together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $23.31 per share. Against a current share price of $8.25, this implies a 64.6% discount, which points to IHS Holding trading well below this DCF estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests IHS Holding is undervalued by 64.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 58 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for IHS Holding.

Approach 2: IHS Holding Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you are paying per share to the earnings that each share generates. It gives a quick sense of how many years of current earnings the market is willing to pay for.

What counts as a “normal” P/E will vary. Higher growth expectations or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk can pull that multiple down. Context therefore matters when you look at any single P/E number.

IHS Holding currently trades on a P/E of about 4.46x. This is below the Telecom industry average P/E of about 17.23x and below the peer average of around 8.22x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for IHS Holding is 12.28x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

Because the Fair Ratio blends these fundamentals rather than relying only on simple peer or industry comparisons, it can offer a more tailored anchor for your expectations. With a current P/E of 4.46x versus a Fair Ratio of 12.28x, IHS Holding screens as trading below this Fair Ratio based view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your IHS Holding Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St this takes the form of Narratives, where you choose a story for IHS Holding that links your view of the business to a forecast and a Fair Value. This can then be compared directly with the current price to help you decide if, when, and at what level you might want to act.

A Narrative is your concise story for a company, written in plain language, that sits behind the numbers you pick for future revenue, earnings and margins. The platform then connects that story to a full set of forecasts and a Fair Value estimate that updates as new information such as earnings or deal news is added.

On the Simply Wall St Community page, Narratives are easy to use and read. For IHS Holding you can see, for example, a more optimistic Narrative that arrives at a Fair Value around US$12.00 and a more cautious Narrative closer to US$7.50. This shows how two investors can look at the same company, use different assumptions, and reach very different yet clearly explained views on what the shares are worth to them.

For IHS Holding however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading IHS Holding Narratives:

🐂 IHS Holding Bull Case

Fair value: US$9.00 per share

Implied discount to this narrative: about 8.3% below fair value at the recent US$8.25 share price

Revenue growth assumption: 8.6% a year

  • Analysts building this case expect solid revenue growth supported by mobile data demand, 4G and 5G roll out and more equipment on existing sites in markets such as Nigeria and Brazil.
  • They see efficiency gains, lower funding costs and debt reduction supporting free cash flow, with room for shareholder returns once the balance sheet is in better shape.
  • The main watchpoints are currency swings in key markets, reliance on a small group of telecom customers, leverage, and the risk that new technologies or regulation could chip away at tower economics over time.

🐻 IHS Holding Bear Case

Fair value: US$7.50 per share

Implied premium to this narrative: about 10.5% above fair value at the recent US$8.25 share price

Revenue growth assumption: 9.1% a year

  • This view leans into risks from satellite and alternative connectivity, tighter ESG rules and higher energy related costs, which could pressure tower demand, margins and free cash flow over time.
  • It also focuses on customer concentration in a few large mobile operators, currency volatility against US dollar debt, and competitive or regulatory changes that might weaken pricing power and long term earnings.
  • Supportive factors such as ongoing mobile infrastructure needs, cost control and diversification into other regions are recognised, but are not seen as enough to offset the risk that the agreed MTN cash offer effectively caps upside.

The gap between these Narratives shows how different assumptions on technology, regulation, currency and customer behaviour can lead to quite different views on what counts as a reasonable price for IHS Holding today.

data.title: Proposed MATCH Act Puts ASML China Exposure And Valuation In Focus
data.description:
• A new bipartisan bill in the U.S., the MATCH Act, has been introduced to restrict ASML Holding's export and servicing of advanced lithography tools in China.
  • The legislation directly targets leading edge systems that are key to cutting edge chip production.
  • China is an important market for ASML, so any limitations on shipments or after sales service could affect future revenue mix and customer relationships.

ASML Holding (NasdaqGS:ASML) supplies lithography equipment that chipmakers use to pattern semiconductors, including the most advanced nodes. The company sits at the center of global chip production capacity, with its tools used across foundries, memory producers, and integrated device manufacturers. With governments increasingly focused on chip supply chains, export rules have become an important input for investors following ASML.

The MATCH Act adds another layer of potential policy risk on top of existing export controls affecting high end tools going to China. Readers may want to watch how the legislative process unfolds, which product lines could be covered, and how ASML communicates any operational or contractual adjustments with customers in China and elsewhere.

Margins Soft, One Offs Still Cloud Headline Profit

  • Over the last 12 months, Bank of Queensland recorded net income of A$98 million on A$1.7b of revenue, which translates to a 5.9% net margin compared with 19.2% a year earlier. That period also includes a one off loss of A$195 million.
  • Consensus narrative expects simplification and digital upgrades to improve margins over time. However, the current 5.9% margin and the A$195 million one off loss put a clear test in front of that bullish view.
    • The focus on higher return commercial lending and digital mortgages aims to lift profitability, but recent net income of A$98 million on A$1.7b of revenue shows that the earnings base is still relatively thin.
    • Plans to improve net interest margin by reshaping the branch network sit alongside a cost to income ratio of 64.7% in the trailing period. This leaves less room for error if execution on simplification or technology projects is slower or more expensive than expected.

Credit Quality Holds While Loan Book Shrinks

  • Non performing loans moved from A$103 million in H2 2024 to A$95 million in H1 2025 and A$94 million in H2 2025, while total loans went from A$80.7b to A$79.6b then A$78.1b over the same halves.
  • Analysts' consensus view highlights a push toward higher returning commercial sectors such as health care and agriculture. The recent pattern of slightly lower non performing loans alongside a smaller loan book shows credit quality metrics holding up even as the mix is being repositioned.
    • The shift away from lower return home lending and toward targeted commercial segments aligns with the move from A$103 million to A$94 million in non performing loans, which may reflect a tighter approach to risk on the existing book.
    • At the same time, a smaller total loan balance of A$78.1b in H2 2025 means growth ambitions in the higher return areas will need to be weighed against the risk that loan demand or credit provisions change as the macro backdrop shifts.

    • Bears also note that the DCF fair value of A$4.66 sits well under A$6.61. If forecast margin gains or cost savings from digital and simplification efforts take longer than hoped, the valuation gap could become harder to justify.

Approach 1: KB Home Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model looks at the value of a stock by projecting all future dividend payments and discounting them back to today. It is essentially asking what those future cash returns to shareholders are worth in present terms.

For KB Home, the model uses a dividend per share of about US$1.01, a return on equity of 15.73% and a payout ratio of 10.92%. Based on these inputs, dividend growth is capped at 3.41%, even though the broader growth input before capping is 14.01%. This cap is intended to keep the long term dividend growth assumption more restrained and potentially more sustainable.

Putting these pieces together, the DDM produces an estimated intrinsic value of US$16.73 per share. What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to be associated with a lower P/E.

data.title: Why You Might Be Interested In Grupo SBF S.A. (BVMF:SBFG3) For Its Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
Readers hoping to buy Grupo SBF S.A. (BVMF:SBFG3) for its dividend will need to make their move shortly, as the stock is about to trade ex-dividend. The ex-dividend date is two business days before a company's record date in most cases, which is the date on which the company determines which shareholders are entitled to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Meaning, you will need to purchase Grupo SBF's shares before the 27th of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 31st of December.

The company's next dividend payment will be R$0.5428511 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed R$0.55 to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Grupo SBF stock has a trailing yield of around 4.2% on the current share price of R$13.17. Dividends are a major contributor to investment returns for long term holders, but only if the dividend continues to be paid. So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. That's why it's good to see Grupo SBF paying out a modest 39% of its earnings. Yet cash flow is typically more important than profit for assessing dividend sustainability, so we should always check if the company generated enough cash to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 58% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

See our latest analysis for Grupo SBF

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. That's why it's comforting to see Grupo SBF's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 24% per annum for the past five years.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Grupo SBF has delivered an average of 59% per year annual increase in its dividend, based on the past three years of dividend payments. It's exciting to see that both earnings and dividends per share have grown rapidly over the past few years.

Final Takeaway

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Grupo SBF? From a dividend perspective, we're encouraged to see that earnings per share have been growing, the company is paying out less than half of its earnings, and a bit over half its free cash flow. It's a promising combination that should mark this company worthy of closer attention.

data.description:

  • The stock is up 0.6% over the last week, has a 6.2% decline over 30 days, yet sits on gains of 19.7% year to date and 31.3% over the past year. This can change how the market is currently assessing both its potential and its risks.

Sector headlines around commodity prices, supply expectations, and capital allocation policies continue to influence how investors think about companies like Coterra Energy.

Approach 1: Coterra Energy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return.

For Coterra Energy, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on current last twelve months free cash flow of about $1.58b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates point to free cash flow of $3.03b by 2030, with annual projections between 2026 and 2035 ranging roughly from $2.60b to $3.39b before discounting. Simply Wall St only uses direct analyst inputs for up to five years, then extends the series using its own growth assumptions.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $93.85 per share. Compared with a current share price around $31.85, this output suggests the stock is 66.1% undervalued according to this DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Coterra Energy Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful quick check because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is already generating. A higher P/E typically reflects stronger growth expectations or lower perceived risk, while a lower P/E can point to more modest growth expectations or higher risk.

Coterra Energy currently trades on a P/E of 14.09x. That sits close to both the Oil and Gas industry average P/E of 15.06x and the peer group average of 13.29x, so on simple comparisons the stock is in the same general range as its sector and similar companies.

Simply Wall St also estimates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Coterra Energy of 22.07x. This Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a basic peer or industry comparison because it factors in elements such as the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, market capitalization, industry, and risk characteristics. By bringing those pieces together, it aims to show what a more company specific P/E could reasonably look like.
